segments.title: Soviet Navy transition from wartime constraints to missile presence
segments.summary: The Soviet Navy is traced from constrained wartime fleets and German-influenced submarines to Khrushchev-era missile submarines and Gorshkov's surface-presence fleet.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames 1939-65 as a period shaped by World War II, Stalin, Kuznetsov, Khrushchev, and Gorshkov. The note follows the lecture's broad arc from the Baltic and Black Sea limits of Soviet sea power to S-class submarine experience, postwar rebuilding, cruise-missile submarines, and the later need for warfighting ships that could also signal status abroad.

evidence.note: Introduces the 1939-65 period as a compressed overview shaped by World War II, Stalin, Kuznetsov, Khrushchev, Gorshkov, submarines, and surface presence.
Explains how Baltic access and newly acquired ports mattered to Kuznetsov, then uses German-influenced S-class submarines to start the Soviet submarine-development thread.
Connects early Soviet cruise-missile submarines and harbor-attack pressure to Khrushchev's choices, the Cuban Missile Crisis, and limits of Soviet surface strength.
Describes the emergence of Kresta-style surface ships and the broader shift toward a fleet that could fight wars and conduct global naval presence.
